question: In mammals, which blood vessel would normally carry largest amount of urea? (NEET-I 2016)
explanation: Urea is produced in the liver and enters the circulation through the hepatic vein, which therefore carries the highest concentration of urea before it is diluted or filtered by the kidneys.

question: Which one of the following animals has two separate circulatory pathways? (2015)
explanation: Whales are mammals and possess a four-chambered heart with two separate circulatory pathways: pulmonary circulation (to the lungs) and systemic circulation (to the rest of the body).

question: Doctors use stethoscope to hear the sounds produced during each cardiac cycle. The second sound is heard when (2015)
options: AV node receives signal from SA node
Ventricular walls vibrate due to gushing in of blood from atria
Semilunar valves close down after the blood flows into vessels from ventricles.
explanation: The second heart sound ('dup') is caused by the closure of the semilunar valves at the beginning of ventricular diastole, preventing the backflow of blood from the aorta and pulmonary artery into the ventricles.

question: Erythropoiesis starts in (2015 Cancelled)
explanation: In adults, erythropoiesis (the production of red blood cells) primarily occurs in the red bone marrow of certain bones.

question: Which one of the following is correct? (2015 Cancelled)
explanation: Blood is composed of plasma and formed elements, which include red blood cells (RBCs), white blood cells (WBCs), and platelets.

question: Blood pressure in the mammalian aorta is maximum during (2015 Cancelled)
explanation: Systolic blood pressure is the maximum pressure recorded in the arteries during the contraction (systole) of the left ventricle, which pumps blood into the aorta.

question: Person with blood group AB is considered as universal recipient because he has (2014)
options: both A and B antigens on RBC but no antibodies in the plasma
both A and B antibodies in the plasma
no antigen on RBC and no antibody in the plasma
both A and B antigens in the plasma but no antibodies.
explanation: Individuals with blood group AB have both A and B antigens on their red blood cells and lack both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in their plasma, allowing them to receive blood from any donor.

question: How do parasympathetic neural signals affect the working of the heart? (2014)
options: Reduce both heart rate and cardiac output.
Heart rate is increased without affecting the cardiac output.
Both heart rate and cardiac output increase.
Heart rate decreases but cardiac output increases.
explanation: Parasympathetic signals, primarily via the vagus nerve, release acetylcholine which slows the heart rate and decreases the strength of contraction, thereby reducing cardiac output.

question: Which one of the following statements is correct regarding blood pressure? (2011)
options: 130/90 mm Hg is considered high and requires treatment.
100/55 mm Hg is considered an ideal blood pressure.
105/50 mm Hg makes one very active.
190/110 mm Hg may harm vital organs like brain and kidney.
explanation: Severe hypertension, such as a reading of 190/110 mm Hg, puts extreme stress on the vascular system and can lead to organ damage in the brain (stroke) and kidneys.

question: A person with unknown blood group under ABO system, has suffered much blood loss in an accident and needs immediate blood transfusion. His friend who has valid certificate of his own blood type, offers for blood donation without delay. What would have been the type of blood group of the donor friend? (2011)
explanation: Blood group O is considered the universal donor because it lacks A and B antigens, meaning it will not react with anti-A or anti-B antibodies in the recipient's plasma.

question: Listed below are four statements (i–iv) regarding human blood circulatory system. (i) Arteries are thick-walled and have narrow lumen as compared to veins. (ii) Angina is acute chest pain when no enough oxygen is reaching the chest muscles. (iii) Person with blood group AB can donate blood to any person with any blood group under ABO system. (iv) Calcium ions play a very important role in blood clotting. Which two of the above statements are correct? (Mains 2010)
explanation: Statement (i) is correct; arteries have thicker muscular walls to withstand high pressure. Statement (iv) is correct; calcium ions (Factor IV) are essential for the coagulation cascade. Statement (ii) is incorrect because angina involves heart muscles, and (iii) is incorrect as AB is the universal recipient, not donor.

question: The haemoglobin content per 100 ml of blood of a normal healthy human adult is (Mains 2010)
explanation: The standard range for haemoglobin in a healthy adult is approximately 12 to 16 grams per 100 ml (deciliter) of blood.

question: There is no DNA in (2009)
explanation: Mature mammalian red blood cells lose their nucleus and other organelles to maximize space for carrying haemoglobin, and thus lack DNA.

question: In a standard ECG which one of the following alphabets is the correct representation of the respective activity of the human heart? (2009)
explanation: The P-wave in a standard electrocardiogram represents the electrical excitation (depolarisation) of the atria, which leads to atrial contraction.

question: Globulins contained in human blood plasma are primarily involved in (2009)
explanation: Gamma globulins (immunoglobulins) are a class of plasma proteins that function as antibodies, playing a vital role in the body's immune defense.

question: Which type of white blood cells are concerned with the release of histamine and the natural anticoagulant heparin? (2008)
explanation: Basophils are granulocytes that secrete heparin (anticoagulant) and histamine (vasodilator), which are involved in inflammatory and allergic reactions.

question: A drop of each of the following, is placed separately on four slides. Which of them will not coagulate? (2007)
options: Sample from the thoracic duct of lymphatic system
explanation: Serum is the liquid part of blood that remains after a clot has formed; it lacks clotting factors like fibrinogen and therefore cannot coagulate again.

question: Which one of the following has an open circulatory system? (2006)
explanation: Cockroaches (Periplaneta) have an open circulatory system where blood (haemolymph) flows freely through body cavities (haemocoel) rather than being confined to vessels.

question: You are required to draw blood from a patient and to keep it in a test tube for analysis of blood corpuscles and plasma. You are also provided with the following four types of test tubes. Which of these you will not use for the purpose? (2004)
explanation: Note: According to the source key, the answer is (c). Standard biological practice uses heparin to prevent clotting to obtain plasma, whereas avoiding it allows clotting to obtain serum. If the goal is plasma analysis, heparin is typically used.

question: The cardiac pacemaker in a patient fails to function normally. The doctors find that an artificial pacemaker is to be grafted in him. It is likely that it will be grafted at the site of (2003)
explanation: The sinuatrial (SA) node is the natural pacemaker of the heart, located in the right atrium, which initiates the electrical impulses for heartbeat.

question: Bundle of His is a network of (2003)
options: muscle fibres distributed throughout the heart walls
muscle fibres found only in the ventricle wall
nerve fibres found throughout the heart.
explanation: The Bundle of His consists of specialized muscle fibres that conduct electrical impulses from the AV node into the ventricular walls.

question: In the ABO system of blood groups, if both antigens are present but no antibody, the blood group of the individual would be (2003)
explanation: Blood group AB is characterized by having both A and B antigens on the surface of red blood cells and having no anti-A or anti-B antibodies in the plasma.

question: Impulse of heart beat originates from (2002)
explanation: The heartbeat is initiated by a specialized patch of nodal tissue called the sinuatrial node (SA node).

question: Which of the following statements is true for lymph? (2002)
explanation: Lymph is essentially filtered blood plasma that has leaked out of capillaries; it contains WBCs but lacks RBCs, platelets, and large plasma proteins.

question: What is correct for blood group O? (2001)
options: No antigens but both a and b antibodies are present
A antigen and b antibody present
A and B antigens and a, b antibodies present
explanation: Blood group O is defined by the absence of A and B antigens on the RBC surface and the presence of both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in the plasma.

question: Difference between pulmonary artery and pulmonary vein is that, the pulmonary artery has (2000)
explanation: Arteries (including the pulmonary artery) generally have thicker, more muscular walls compared to veins to handle higher blood pressure.

question: In which point, pulmonary artery is different from pulmonary vein? (2000)
explanation: Refer to the explanation for the previous question.

question: Which statement is true for WBC? (2000)
explanation: White blood cells exhibit diapedesis, the ability to squeeze through the narrow gaps between the endothelial cells of capillary walls to reach sites of infection.

question: Rate of heart beat is determined by (1999)
explanation: The SA node acts as the primary pacemaker, setting the basic rhythm and rate of the heart's contraction.

question: Which is the principal cation in the plasma of the blood? (1999)
explanation: Sodium (Na+) is the most abundant positively charged ion (cation) in the extracellular fluid and blood plasma.

question: The blood group, with antibody-A and antibody- B is (1999)
explanation: Blood group O contains both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in its plasma.

question: The thickening of walls of arteries is called (1999)
explanation: Arteriosclerosis is a general term for the thickening and hardening of the walls of the arteries, often occurring with age or disease.

question: An adult human with average health has systolic and diastolic pressures as (1998)
explanation: Standard normal blood pressure for a healthy adult is typically around 120 mm Hg (systolic) over 80 mm Hg (diastolic).

question: Which of the following is not the main function of lymph glands? (1998)
explanation: Lymph nodes (glands) are sites of immune activity (WBC production and filtration); they do not produce red blood cells, which are made in the bone marrow.

question: Which of the following is agranulocyte? (1997)
explanation: Lymphocytes and monocytes are agranulocytes, meaning they lack visible granules in their cytoplasm, unlike neutrophils, basophils, and eosinophils.

question: The life span of human WBC is approximately (1997)
explanation: Most white blood cells have a relatively short life span, often ranging from a few days to less than two weeks, depending on the type and body needs.

question: The correct route through which pulse-making impulse travels in the heart is (1995)
options: SA node → Purkinje fibres → bundle of His → AV node → heart muscles
SA node → AV node → bundle of His → Purkinje fibres → heart muscles
AV node → bundle of His → SA node → Purkinje fibres → heart muscles
AV node → SA node → Purkinje fibres → bundle of His → heart muscles
explanation: The conduction sequence is: SA node → Atria → AV node → Bundle of His → Purkinje fibres → Ventricular muscles.

question: The lymph serves to (1995)
options: return the interstitial fluid to the blood
return the WBCs and RBCs to the lymph nodes
explanation: The lymphatic system collects excess fluid (interstitial fluid) from the tissues and returns it to the venous circulation.

question: In veins, valves are present to check backward flow of blood flowing at (1995)
explanation: Blood pressure is significantly lower in veins than in arteries. Valves are necessary to ensure one-way flow back to the heart against gravity.

question: The neurogenic heart is the characteristic feature of (1995)
explanation: In most arthropods, the heartbeat is initiated by nervous impulses (neurogenic), unlike the myogenic heart of vertebrates where the impulse originates within the heart muscle itself.

question: The heart sound ‘dup’ is produced when (1994)
options: semi-lunar valves at the base of aorta get closed
explanation: Refer to the explanation for question 8.

question: The pacesetter in the heart is called (1994)
explanation: While the SA node is the primary pacemaker, the AV node is often called the pacesetter as it delays and then relays the impulse to the ventricles.

question: Blood capillaries are made of (1993)
options: endothelium, connective tissue and muscle fibres
explanation: Capillaries are the simplest blood vessels, consisting of only a single layer of squamous endothelial cells.

question: Cells formed in bone marrow include (1992)
explanation: Red bone marrow is the site of production for all types of blood cells, including red blood cells and white blood cells (leucocytes).

question: The genotype of B blood group father of an O blood group child is (1992)
explanation: An O blood group child must have the genotype IOIO. For a father with group B to have such a child, he must be heterozygous, carrying one B allele and one O allele (IBIO).

question: Component of blood responsible for producing antibodies is (1992)
explanation: B-lymphocytes are the specialized white blood cells that produce and secrete antibodies.

question: Blood group AB has (1991)
explanation: Refer to the explanation for question 43.

question: Carbonic anhydrase occurs in (1991)
explanation: Red blood cells contain high concentrations of the enzyme carbonic anhydrase, which facilitates the rapid transport and conversion of CO2.

question: Wall of blood capillary is formed of (1991)
explanation: Refer to the explanation for question 64.

question: Removal of calcium from freshly collected blood would (1989)
explanation: Calcium is a vital clotting factor. Agents that remove or bind calcium (like oxalates or EDTA) prevent the blood from clotting.

question: A person with blood group A requires blood. The blood group which can be given is (1989)
explanation: A person with blood group A can receive blood from donors of group A or group O (the universal donor).

question: Tricuspid valve is found in between (1989)
explanation: The tricuspid valve is the right atrioventricular valve, located between the right atrium (auricle) and the right ventricle.

question: RBCs do not occur in (1988)
explanation: Insects like cockroaches have an open circulatory system and their 'blood' (haemolymph) does not contain red blood cells or respiratory pigments.
